analogy: Think of "Democracy and Diversity" through the chapter's own opening images: G e n d e r , R e l i g i o n a n d C a s t e 29 Chapter 3 Gender, Religion and Caste Overview The existence of social diversity does not threaten democracy. Political expression of social differences... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: (a) B C A D, (b) B A D C, (c) D C A B, (d) C A B D. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Democracy and Diversity is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that g e n d e r , R e l i g i o n a n d C a s t e 29 Chapter 3 Gender, Religion and Caste Overview The existence of social diversity does not threaten democracy.

The text explains that political expression of social differences is possible and sometimes quite desirable in a democratic system.

The text explains that in this chapter we apply these ideas to the practice of democracy in India.

The text explains that we look at three kinds of social differences that can take the form of social divisions and inequalities.

The text explains that these are social differences based on gender, religion and caste.

The text explains that in each case we look at the nature of this division in India and how it gets expressed in politics.

The text explains that we also ask whether different expressions based on these differences are healthy or otherwise in a democracy.

The text explains that 30 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s Gender and politics Let us begin with gender division.

The text explains that this is a form of hierarchical social division seen everywhere, but is rarely recognised in the study of politics.

The text explains that the gender division tends to be understood as natural and unchangeable.

The text explains that however, it is not based on biology but on social expectations and stereotypes.

The text explains that public/private division Boys and girls are brought up to believe that the main responsibility of women is housework and bringing up children.

The text explains that this is reflected in a sexual division of labour in most families: women do all work inside the home such as cooking, cleaning, washing clothes, tailoring, looking after children, etc., and men do all the work outside the home.

The text explains that it is not that men cannot do housework; they simply think that it is for women to attend to these things.

The text explains that when these jobs are paid for, men are ready to take up these works.

The text explains that similarly, it is not that women do not work outside their home.

The text explains that women in different parts of the world organised and agitated for equal rights.

The text explains that there were agitations in different countries for the extension of voting rights to women.

The text explains that sexual division of labour: A system in which all work inside the home is either done by the women of the family, or organised by them through the domestic helpers.

The text explains that political expression of gender division and political mobilisation Discuss all these perceptions of an ideal woman that prevail in our society.

The text explains that feminist: A woman or a man who believes in equal rights and opportunities for women and men. © Zuban on this question helped to improve women’s role in public life.

The text explains that 32 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s Patriarchy: Literally, rule by father, this concept is used to refer to a system that values men more and gives them power over women.

The text explains that 12:25 11:10 Daily time use (hours: minutes) Source: Government of India, Time Use Survey, 1998-99. + A ‘time use survey’ was conducted in six states of our country.

The text explains that women also do a lot of direct income generating work, but the bulk of their work is household related.

The text explains that ours is still a male-dominated, patriarchal society.

The text explains that but they drop out because parents prefer to spend their resources for their boys’ education rather than spending equally on their sons and daughters.  No wonder the proportion of women among the highly paid and valued jobs, is still very small.

The text explains that in this respect, India is among the Could you think of some reasons why women’s representation is so low in India?

The text explains that women in national parliaments in different regions of the world (in%) Note: Figures are for the per cent of women in the directly elected houses of parliament as on 1 October 2018.

The text explains that source: http://archive.ipu.org/wmn-e/world.htm Nordic countries Americas Europe Sub- Saharan Africa Pacific Arab States Region Asia 0 20 15 10 5 World Average 24 India 45 40 35 30 25 29.5 42.3 26.4 23.7 19.8 18.6 15.6 11.8

The text explains that why don’t we oppose all those who divide the society on any lines – caste, religion or gender? bottom group of nations in the world (see the graph below).

The text explains that in the government, cabinets are largely all-male even when a woman becomes the Chief Minister or the Prime Minister.

The text explains that one-third of seats in local government bodies – in panchayats and municipalities – are now reserved for women.

The text explains that he believed that politics must be guided by ethics drawn from religion.  Human rights groups in our country have demanded that the Government should take special steps to prevent communal riots and protect religious minorities.  Women’s movement has argued that family laws of all religions discriminate against women.

The text explains that so they have demanded that government should change these laws to make them more equitable.

The text explains that the example of Northern Ireland in Chapter 3 shows the dangers of such an approach to nationalism.

The text explains that the makers of our Constitution were aware of this challenge.

The text explains that this choice was reflected in several constitutional provisions that we studied last year:  There is no official religion for the Indian state.

The text explains that 38 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s in Pakistan and that of Christianity in England, our Constitution does not give a special status to any religion.  The Constitution provides to all individuals and communities freedom to profess, practice and propagate any religion, or not to follow any.  The Constitution prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion.  At the same time, the Constitution allows the state to intervene in the matters of religion in order to ensure equality within religious communities.

The text explains that a secular Constitution like ours is necessary but not sufficient to combat communalism.

The text explains that what makes it different from other societies is that in this system, hereditary occupational division was sanctioned by rituals.

The text explains that caste system was based on exclusion of and discrimination against the ‘outcaste’ groups.

The text explains that ambedkar and Periyar Ramaswami Naicker advocated and worked to establish a society in which caste inequalities are absent.

The text explains that partly due to their efforts and partly due to other socio-economic changes, castes and caste system in modern India have undergone great changes.

The text explains that the Scheduled Tribes, often referred to as Adivasis, include those communities that led a secluded life usually in hills and forests and did not interact much with the rest of society.

The text explains that population of different religious communities in India, 2011 Hindu 79.8 Muslim 14.2 Others 2% Christian 2.3 Sikh 1.7 Others include Buddhist 0.7% Jain 0.4% Other Religions and Persuasions 0.7% Religion not stated 0.2% Source: Census of India, 2011 In %

The text explains that the Constitution of India prohibited any caste-based discrimination and laid the foundations of policies to reverse the injustices of the caste system.

The text explains that untouchability has not ended completely, despite constitutional prohibition.

The text explains that the caste groups that had access to education under the old system have done very well in acquiring modern education as well.

The text explains that when governments are formed, political parties usually take care that representatives of different castes and tribes find a place in it.  Political parties and candidates in elections make appeals to caste sentiment to muster support.

The text explains that it (a) prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion.

The text explains that (c) provides to all individuals freedom to profess any religion.

The text explains that (d) ensures equality of citizens within religious communities.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II, Chapter 3: Democracy and Diversity

prelims_traps: Do not treat (a) B C A D and (b) B A D C as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Democracy and Diversity; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Democracy and Diversity through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use (a) B C A D and (b) B A D C to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

In this chapter we apply these ideas to the practice of democracy in India.

options: In villages, women fetch water, collect fuel and work in the fields.

In fact, the majority of women do some sort of paid work in addition to domestic labour.
But their work is not valued and does not get recognition.
